exemplar.title:
Research question: How does the length of a string influence the rate of mechanical 
energy dissipated in a simple pendulum system?

detailedExemplarData.title:
Research question: How does the length of a string influence the rate of mechanical 
energy dissipated in a simple pendulum system?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Raw and processed data tables are clearly labeled with units and uncertainties, aiding transparency.
Instrumental uncertainties for length, angle and mass are stated, reflecting initial planning.
A damping coefficient is extracted via logarithmic analysis, showing appropriate processing methods.
weaknesses: Uncertainties are listed without justification or propagation through derived quantities.
No sample graph is provided despite referencing slope determination, hampering clarity.
Numerical inaccuracies and unit omissions in energy calculations reveal processing inconsistencies.
Error analysis is limited to instrumental uncertainties; no error bars or propagated uncertainties are presented.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data communication is mostly clear, but the absence of error propagation, graphical evidence and some processing inaccuracies constrain this work to a medium band. A score of 3/6 reflects solid presentation overshadowed by significant analytical omissions.

strengths: The research question is situated within a clear, specific context linking pendulum damping to historical time-keeping and energy dissipation.
Controls for mass, release angle and string material are correctly identified and explained, showing solid methodological planning.
The step-by-step procedure is detailed enough to allow reproduction with minimal inference.
Comprehensive materials list and a clear, testable hypothesis demonstrate careful experimental design.
weaknesses: The research question itself is not stated concisely, weakening the focus of the investigation.
Rationale for selecting ten oscillation cycles is absent, reducing reproducibility justification.
Gravity is omitted as a control variable despite its fundamental influence.
Certain procedure steps (release mechanism, video timing software) lack exact detail.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the student provides an excellent, specific background and identifies key controls, the absence of a clearly stated research question and some procedural ambiguities prevent a topâband score. The strong context and detailed design justify a high mark of 5 out of 6.

strengths: The conclusion draws on theoretical concepts of damping, air resistance and frequency to interpret the results.
Findings are linked qualitatively to the data, showing an attempt to justify observations.
There is some comparison to accepted scientific ideas (damping models) in an IB-appropriate context.
weaknesses: The conclusion remains qualitative and lacks quantitative consistency with the data (e.g., anomalous energy-loss trends).
No numeric check or comparison to published damping coefficients is attempted.
Internal inconsistencies (longer pendulum losses sometimes higher) are not discussed.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student provides a relevant conclusion and situates it within a scientific context, but superficial analysis and failure to reconcile inconsistencies restrict this to the midâband. A mark of 3/6 is warranted.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses (air currents, pivot friction, mass distribution) are identified and described.
Realistic and relevant improvements (vacuum chamber, low-friction bearings, statistical justification of repeats) are proposed.
Links between weaknesses and their effects on energy dissipation are articulated.
weaknesses: The relative impact of each identified weakness is not fully explained or quantified.
Some suggested improvements (safety section) remain overly generic rather than tightly focused.
Explanations of how new equipment would improve precision lack detailed rationale.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation shows clear identification of concrete limitations and practical improvements, fitting the top of the 3-4 band. The absence of a deeper impact analysis prevents a maximum score, justifying a 4/6.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student situates the research question within the clear and specific context of energy dissipation in pendulums, linking it to time-keeping, damping forces, and length dependence.  The background section shows why the phenomenon is interesting and defines the variables to be studied; hence the context is both appropriate and specific, meeting the descriptor for the 5-6 band, albeit with minor stylistic lapses that justify awarding the lower mark of 5 within the band.

Only instrumental uncertainties are stated (Â±1Â°, Â±0.5 cm, Â±2 g).  There is no propagation, no graphical error bars, and no discussion of overall uncertainty in damping coefficient or energy loss.  Evidence of consideration is therefore limited, matching the 1-2 band; the clearer listing of instrumental errors justifies the upper mark of 2.

The student identifies relevant controls (mass, release angle, string material) and explains their influence, showing an attempt to ensure data are sufficient to address the question.  However, several aspectsâpivot friction, ambient conditions, number of cyclesâare not considered until the evaluation, and rationale for 10-cycle limit is absent.  Thus the considerations are described but not fully explained, fitting 3-4 band; a mid-band mark of 3 is appropriate.

Specific weaknesses (air currents, pivot friction, mass distribution) are identified and briefly linked to how they could skew energy dissipation, though discussion of *relative* impact is not deeply developed.  This meets âdescribes specific methodological weaknessesâ, top of 3-4 band (4).

The step-by-step procedure (string measurement, mounting, release method, video recording, repetitions) would allow another investigator to reproduce the work with only a few ambiguities (e.g. how the lift is positioned, how time is obtained from the video).  Detail on timing/angle extraction software is thin, so full replication would need some inference.  These features correspond to the 3-4 band; the clearer parts merit the top of that band (4).

Angles are averaged, amplitudes and sample energy calculations are provided, and a damping coefficient is extracted from a logarithmic plot.  However, several numerical errors (e.g. amplitude table inconsistencies, unexplained jump in energy series) reveal significant inaccuracies/omissions.  That fits the 3-4 descriptor (âsome significant omissionsâ); a score of 3 is appropriate.

Improvements such as using a vacuum chamber, better instruments and low-friction bearings are realistic and relevant, but the explanation of how each would quantitatively enhance data is limited.  Therefore the work fits the 3-4 band; a mid-band score of 3 is assigned.

Numerous tables of raw and processed data are included and labelled.  Despite formatting glitches, values, units and uncertainties are generally visible, so communication is clear; precision is occasionally lost (mixed units in amplitude table), preventing the highest band.  This aligns with âeither clear or preciseâ, warranting the upper end of the 3-4 band.

The student references theoretical ideas of damping, frequency and air resistance to justify findings, but comparison remains qualitative and somewhat superficial; no numeric check against accepted damping models is attempted.  Hence âsome relevant comparisonâ (3-4 band) with a score of 3.

A qualitative conclusion is drawn and linked to the processed data, but internal inconsistencies (energy losses for 30 cm sometimes higher than shorter cords) and lack of quantitative comparison mean the conclusion is not fully consistent with the analysis.  This corresponds to âdescribed but not fully consistentâ, i.e. the 3-4 band; mid-band mark 3 given.

annotations.comment: The rationale for measuring ten oscillations is not explained. Include justification for cycle count to ensure reproducibility.
Controls for air resistance and pivot friction are correctly identified, demonstrating methodological thought.
Variable table contains meta-questions and notes that should be removed for clarity and professionalism.
Gravity is not listed as a control variable despite its direct impact on pendulum motion; it should be noted.
The introduction effectively situates the pendulumâs historical significance and the gap in understanding length-dependent energy loss.
The student does not explicitly state a focused research question. A clear, concise question would anchor the investigation.
The inline formula repeats ``T = 2Ïâ(L/a)`` instead of using the correct gravitational constant g. Correct the variable.
Background section clearly explains amplitude decay and energy dissipation mechanisms with appropriate examples.
The hypothesis is clear and testable, linking length inversely to energy dissipation rate.
The materials list is comprehensive and demonstrates careful planning of experimental resources.
The procedure step describing use of phone as flashlight is unconventional and may introduce inconsistent lighting conditions.
The release mechanism is vaguely described; specify exactly how the pendulum is released without imparting additional impulse.
Safety considerations list generic precautions but note that the assembly involves no chemical hazards; streamline this section.
Raw data tables are clearly labeled with trial numbers and uncertainties, aiding transparency.
Uncertainty of Â±1Â° is listed but lacks justification of protractor resolution; include calibration details.
Sample size of three repeats is standard but the choice should be justified statistically (e.g., confidence level).
Preliminary measurement uncertainties are clearly stated for length and mass, reflecting good experimental planning.
The slope determination process refers to a plot but no example graph or data points are shown; include a sample plot.
Equations for potential and kinetic energy are referenced but not explicitly written; include full expressions for U and K.
Duplicate display of the exponential decay equation appears; streamline to a single clear presentation.
No error propagation is shown for derived quantities like damping coefficient; propagate uncertainties through calculations.
The energy dissipation calculation example uses numerical values but skips units in the final subtraction; always include J.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
